title: Network Settings for Improved Performance
breadcrumbs: Deployment Considerations for Performance and Scalability
text: Network Settings for Improved Performance It is possible to adjust network loads by configuring the network connections. There are several connector settings, command line switches, and assignment settings that you can use to fine-tune the network connection for optimal performance. TCP Connector Options sb , rb , ub —match the TCP/IP send, received, and message buffer sizes to that of Uniface. For more information, see sb , rb , and ub . n —limit the number of network packages by turning nagling on. For more information, see n . Assignment Settings $NET_TIMEOUT —Specify the time that the Uniface Router should try to reestablish a connection over TCP when a connection to a server fails. For more information, see $NET_TIMEOUT . $NET_FMT —Specify the format of the hit data that is returned from the server to the client. For more information, see $NET_FMT . Command Line Switches /maxidle —Specify the maximum time the specified server is allowed to be idle (not handling any requests). For more information, see /maxidle . Tuning Network Connections Edit the Uniface Router assignment file urouter.asn to specify the maximum idle time and number of reconnection attempts for the server. In the [SERVERS] section, set the maximum idle time of the shared server using /maxidle . It is recommended that you specify a wait period suitable for your working environment. For example, use /maxidle=8h to specify eight hours. If /maxidle is not specified, the server continues running regardless of how long the clients are inactive. In the [SETTINGS] section, use $NET_TIMEOUT to specify the client connect timeout, server connect timeout, and the idle timeout. Edit the [SETTINGS] section your assignment file. Specify the format of the hit data that is returned from the server to the client using NET_FMT . Use the USYS$TCP_PARAMS setting to match the TCP/IP buffer size to that of Uniface. Uniface uses message buffers of 8192 bytes maximum for sending data packets, but some TCP/IP implementations cannot handle this size of buffer. A maximum record setting of 4096 bytes is usually appropriate, but you should reduce the maximum to the lowest maximum value supported in the network, and on both the client and server sides of the network. For example, if the client uses a maximum buffer size of 1024 bytes, and the server can support up to 4096 bytes, set ub=1024 on both sides. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
